coredata.dc:title: Impact of nitrogen doping on the polarization properties of carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Siyuan
Liu, Xingchen
Li, Sen
Tan, Yonggen
Yu, Jingyan
Zhang, Chao
Feng, Jun
coredata.dc:description: This study investigated the polarization characteristics of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and their correlation with nitrogen doping levels through experimental and theoretical approaches. We compared carbon quantum dots with different nitrogen doping contents and discovered that with increasing nitrogen content, the lattice dimensions of the nitrogen-doped CQDs decrease while their anisotropy becomes more distinct. These changes resulted in significant variations in polarization and phase. Furthermore, the introduction of nitrogen elements was found to influence the fluorescence emission properties of the CQDs as well as the hybridization state of the carbon atoms. The findings underscored the potential to tailor the polarization properties of carbon quantum dots through controlled nitrogen doping, opening up new avenues for their application in advanced display technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Nitrogen-dopping
Polarized emission

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge financial support from Southern University of Science and Technology and Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory Program under contract no 2021B1212040001 . This work was also supported by the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2021M701589 ). The authors thank Prof.Qihe Wei, Dr.Shufang Gao, Dr. Tao Lei, Mr.Zheng Zhang, Mr Zhiyuan Huang for insights and discussions on materials synthesis. And we also acknowledge the assistance of SUSTech Core Research Facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram showing the nitrogen-doped CQDs synthesis.
(a) XRD patterns of the different nitrogen doping content of nitrogen-doped CQDs; (b) Raman spectra of the different nitrogen doping content of nitrogen-doped CQDs.
HRTEM images of (a) low nitrogen-doped content CQDs; (b) high nitrogen-doped content CQDs.
FTIR spectra of the different nitrogen doping content of nitrogen-doped CQDs.
Full XPS spectra and high-resolution spectra of C 1s and N 1s for (a) low nitrogen-doped content CQDs; (b) medium nitrogen-doped content CQDs and (c) high nitrogen-doped content CQDs.
Electron distribution diagram of graphitic nitrogen (a) and pyridinic nitrogen (b) in CQDs; Theoretical calculations of absorption in graphitic nitrogen (c) and pyridinic nitrogen (d) in CQDs.

Optical properties of the different nitrogen doping content of nitrogen-doped CQDs, indicating the corresponding UV–Vis, PL emissions, and PL excitation spectra of the (a) low nitrogen-doped content CQDs, (b) medium nitrogen-doped content CQDs, and (c) high nitrogen-doped content CQDs; excitation-emission matrix for the (d) low nitrogen-doped content CQDs, (e) medium nitrogen-doped content CQDs, and (f) high nitrogen-doped content CQDs.
Schematic showing the experimental polarization setup.

(a) The polarization properties of the different nitrogen doping content of nitrogen-doped CQDs; (b) PL intensity versus the excitation polarization of the different nitrogen doping content of nitrogen-doped CQDs.
XPS data analysis of weight ratios of the N 1s spectra of the different nitrogen doping content of nitrogen-doped CQDs.
The luminescence lifetime and Quantum Yield of the different nitrogen doping content of nitrogen-doped CQDs.

head.ce:title: Impact of nitrogen doping on the polarization properties of carbon quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering
Southern University of Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigated the polarization characteristics of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and their correlation with nitrogen doping levels through experimental and theoretical approaches. We compared carbon quantum dots with different nitrogen doping contents and discovered that with increasing nitrogen content, the lattice dimensions of the nitrogen-doped CQDs decrease while their anisotropy becomes more distinct. These changes resulted in significant variations in polarization and phase. Furthermore, the introduction of nitrogen elements was found to influence the fluorescence emission properties of the CQDs as well as the hybridization state of the carbon atoms. The findings underscored the potential to tailor the polarization properties of carbon quantum dots through controlled nitrogen doping, opening up new avenues for their application in advanced display technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The display industry has made notable progress in recent years, meeting the diverse needs of various smart devices such as TVs and computers [1,2]. Since the 1980s, liquid crystal display (LCD) technology has dominated the market [3,4]. However, the backlight has to pass through multiple polarizing layers in LCD systems, leading to significant energy loss and reduced optical efficiency [5–7]. By contrast, semiconductor quantum dots light-emitting diode (LED) systems can directly emit polarized light, and their adjustable optical and high quantum yield meet the market demands for high color purity, optical efficiency, resolution, and wide color gamut [8–12]. However, their potential toxicity and relatively high production costs have urgently motivated the industry to seek alternative luminescent materials. In the early 21st century, Sun et al. accidently discovered carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [13]. Due to their tunable emission colors, high color purity, no toxicity, low cost, and the ability to emit polarized light, CQDs are considered an ideal alternative, offering great potential in the future of display technology [14–18].

Doping CQDs with hereto atoms serves as a crucial method to optimize the optical properties of CQDs, as the participation of hetero atoms can modulate the band gap and electron density of CQDs. Among all elements, the nitrogen atom was first studied as a doped element due to its availability in many compounds, making it highly useful for enhancing the overall conjugation level and increasing the electron transition rate. Nitrogen doping can also introduce energy traps into the band gap, promoting radiative transitions. Research on nitrogen-doped CQDs has shown significant promise in recent years [19–21]. For example, nitrogen-doped CQDs prepared from plants using the hydrothermal method could act as pH probes for water and soil detection [22]. Nitrogen-doped CQDs prepared using hydrothermal treatment have been used for the sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions due to their coordination effect with CQDs and cell imaging [23]. Moreover, the fluorescence-polarized emission of CQDs, as an optical property, can theoretically be controlled by nitrogen doping. However, minimal research has been conducted on this topic.

This study involved synthesizing three types of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with varying nitrogen contents by adjusting the ratio of citric acid to ammonium citrate using a straightforward thermal sintering method. The first type consisted of CQDs doped with low nitrogen content (labeled as LN-CQDs), the second type consisted of CQDs doped with medium nitrogen content (labeled as MN-CQDs), and the last type consisted of CQDs doped with high nitrogen content (labeled as HN-CQDs). The surface structures of these nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots were analyzed through various characterization techniques. Subsequently, the polarized fluorescence emission characteristics of the CQDs with different nitrogen contents were studied. The results showed that the degree of linear polarization significantly increased with increasing nitrogen content. By integrating DFT theoretical calculations, the internal mechanism of polarization generated by nitrogen-doped CQDs could be explained, thereby expanding the application range of CQDs in the field of displays.

Ammonium citrate tribasic (99.99 %), and citric acid (99.99 %) were purchased from Macklin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China), and deionized water was used during the synthesis process. All of the chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used as received.

To synthesize the CQDs with varying nitrogen contents, 1 g of citric acid was mixed with 4 g of ammonium citrate tribasic, 2 g of citric acid with 3 g of ammonium citrate tribasic, along with 4 g of citric acid and 1 g of ammonium citrate tribasic. Each mixture was placed in a crucible and sintered at 180 °C for 4 h. The sintered products were then collected and mixed with deionized water to fabricate a solution with a concentration of 1 mg/ml. The solution was purified using a 1000 Da molecular weight cutoff dialysis membrane, which allowed molecules with a molecular weight of less than 1000 to pass freely through while retaining the larger molecules. The dialysis process was carried out for 48 h, during which the dialysis water was not replaced. The purified solution was fabricated into a powder with freeze dryer and then reconstituted into a solution for subsequent analysis and detection (The freeze-drying process usually lasts 72 h). Fig. 1 shows a schematic diagram of the nitrogen-doped CQDs synthesis process.

The geometric optimization and electron density of the nitrogen-doped CQDs were calculated by spin-unrestricted DFT calculations, which were performed using DMol3 code in Materials Studio (MS) using numerical functions on an atom-centered grid as its atomic basis. The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) using the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) method was utilized as the exchange-correlation functional. The all-electron relativistic set, which included all core electrons, and explicitly introduced some relativistic effects into the core, was adopted for core treatment. Double numerical plus polarization (DNP) was used as the basis set, and 4.4 was used as the basis file. The DFT-D method within the Grimme scheme was adopted for more accurate calculations of the van der Waals (vdW) interactions. The energy convergence tolerance was 1.0 e−5 Ha, the maximum force was 0.002 Ha/Å, the maximum displacement was 0.005 Å, and the global orbit cutoff value was 4.0 Å for geometry optimization.

The fluorescence spectrum of the CQDs is acquired by our home-built fluorescence spectroscopy setup, which consists of the tunable light source TLS-EQ-77 (ENERGETIQ, Japan), fiber optics, sample holder, focus optics, and the CCS 200 spectrophotometer (Thorlabs, USA, Slit (W x H) is 20 μm × 2 mm). The subsequent fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield were measured using the FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). The X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) were shown with a Smartlab X-ray diffractometer at the angle from 10 to 80° (scan speed 10°/min, scan step 0.01°/s, RIGAKU, Japan). Raman spectra were collected on Lab RAM HR Raman microscope (Horiba, Japan). The microstructure was recorded using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Tecnai, F30, 200 KeV, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured on PHI 5000 Versaprobe (ULVAC-PHI, Japan). The XPS spectra were calibrated to an adventitious carbon peak at 284.8 eV. Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy (UV–vis spectra) were measured on Lambda 750s (PerkinElmer, USA), and the UV–vis spectra were also recorded to cross-check the XPS analysis results. The Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR spectra) of solid-state samples (KBr matrix) were measured on a Vertex 70V spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) in the region 1000–4000 cm−1.

The XRD patterns are shown in Fig. 2 (a), indicating that the LN-CQDs, MN-CQDs, and HN-CQDs displayed different diffraction patterns, with notable differences in peak positions and widths. The XRD pattern of the LN-CQDs demonstrated a peak at 2θ = 19.1°, corresponding to a d-spacing value of 0.4666 nm, possibly due to the sp3 hybridized carbon within the graphene layer planar distortions, indicating the predominance of a diamond-like, tightly packed sp3 carbon structure [24]. Conversely, the XRD pattern of the HN-CQDs indicated a peak at 2θ = 26.5°, with a d-spacing value of 0.3377 nm, corresponding to the characteristic (002) diffraction peak of graphite. This indicated that the interlayer stacking of the HN-CQDs was similar to that of graphite, dominated by closely packed, highly conjugated sp2 carbon in the core [25]. In addition, the XRD pattern of MN-CQDs showed a peak centered at 2θ = 22.8° (corresponding to a d-spacing of 0.3897 nm). Given that this interlayer spacing falls between the LN-CQDs and HN-CQDs, it suggested that its core was a transitional structure co-dominated by both sp2 and sp3 carbon.

Fig. 2(b) displays typical Raman spectra for three types of CQDs, showcasing two characteristic peaks at 1355 and 1570 cm−1, which correspond to the disordered (D band) and graphite (G band) structures in carbon materials, respectively. The ratio of ID/IG was characteristic of the extent of graphite and the ratio of sp3/sp2 carbon. The ratios of ID/IG were 0.4395, 0.3997, and 0.3675 for LN-CQDs, MN-CQDs, and HN-CQDs. The decreased ratio value implied the increasing graphite content in the CQDs from LN-CQDs to HN-CQDs, indicating that the graphitization of CQDs was enhanced in HN-CQDs. Therefore, the XRD and Raman results revealed a difference in crystalline structures, significantly influenced by the type and arrangement of carbon hybridization in the core.

The microstructure of LN-CQDs, MN-CQDs, and HN-CQDs were characterized using a transmission electron microscope (TEM). Fig. 3 shows the HRTEM images, illustrating that all nitrogen-doped CQDs exhibited highly crystalline carbon structures. Two types of lattice fringe distances were observed. In LN-CQDs, where exclusively a lattice fringe distance of 0.32 nm was found, and HN-CQDs, where exclusively a lattice fringe distance of 0.22 nm was observed, and both of these lattice fringe distances were discovered in MN-CQDs (as shown in Fig. S1), indicating an increase in nitrogen content leads to a change in the lattice fringe distance, which was consistent with the XRD and Raman results. Differences in nitrogen doping levels led to forming different carbon hybridization types in the CQDs core. At the same time, differences in nitrogen doping levels led to the distortion of the internal CQDs lattices. Therefore, additional measurements will be needed to assess property changes, such as fluorescence emissions, due to varying degrees of nitrogen doping.

To further investigate the structural changes of the nitrogen-doped CQDs, FTIR and XPS were employed for detailed surface analysis. As shown in Fig. 4 , the FTIR spectra revealed that all nitrogen-doped CQDs contained an abundance of oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups. Specifically, the peak at 1515 cm−1 of absorption belonged to the CC stretching vibrations, the peak at 1550 cm−1 corresponded to CN stretching vibrations, the 1690 cm−1 peak was attributed to the CO stretching vibration, and the absorption band centered at 1460 cm−1 corresponded to the C–H bending vibrations [25]. In addition, the peak observed at 3300 cm−1 is characteristic of the N–H stretching vibrations [26]. Overall, for three types of CQDs, no significant shifts in the various peak positions were observed, however, changes in the intensities of the different peaks were quite noticeable. For example, the spectral bands located at 1550 cm−1 and 3300 cm−1 were found to increase in intensity as the nitrogen content increased. This phenomenon not only indicates that the content of CN and N–H groups increases with the increase of nitrogen content but also implies that the degree of conjugation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) increases with the increase of nitrogen content. These spectral changes reflected the difference between different nitrogen doping content of nitrogen-doped CQDs in the chemical composition.

We have conducted theoretical calculations on nitrogen-doped CQDs, referencing specific parameters previously established. Based on previous studies, we used aromatic rings to simulate the CQDs structure [29]. Geometry optimization was performed for every conceivable combination of doping sites, and the total energies for each nitrogen-doped CQD variant, featuring both graphitic and pyridinic nitrogen, are tabulated in Tables S1 and S2. The structure manifesting the lowest total energy was selected as the focus of our simulation (Fig. S2), and its electronic distribution is analyzed in Fig. 6 . Fig. 6(a) illustrates graphitic nitrogen atoms replacing central carbon atoms in the aromatic core, thereby augmenting localized electronic states. In contrast Fig. 6(b) displays pyridinic nitrogen atoms situated along the edges of the CQDs, boosting surface reactivity via lone pair electrons. Moreover, energy band calculations for the two types of CQDs were carried out. These results revealed that graphitic nitrogen-doped CQDs exhibit a narrower energy gap (0.499 eV), in contrast to pyridinic nitrogen-doped CQDs, which exhibit a wider energy gap (1.801 eV). This indicates that graphitic nitrogen doping in CQDs decreases the energy gap, resulting in a red shift in luminescence [30]. Lastly, the calculation absorption peaks for both graphitic and pyridinic nitrogen-doped CQDs, as depicted in Fig. 6(c) and (d), indicate that the integration of graphitic nitrogen induces a red shift in the absorption peak of CQDs relative to pyridinic nitrogen doping.

Further optical property investigation was conducted of the resulting nitrogen-doped CQDs, according to the UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence behavior. As shown in Fig. 7 , all CQDs doped with different nitrogen content displayed two characteristic absorption peaks in the UV–Vis spectra near 350 and 450 nm. These peaks indicated n-π* transitions, which were attributed to the edge states at the boundaries of the sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbons and the electron transition of the surface states [31–33]. A notable disparity between the CQDs with varying nitrogen contents involved the intensity of the absorption peaks, the peaks intensity in the HN-CQDs was highest. The solutions containing LN-CQDs, MN-CQDs, and HN-CQDs demonstrated emission peaks at 462 nm, 474 nm, and 492 nm under an excitation light wavelength of 360 nm. For the HN-CQDs, the emission peak was observed near the longer wavelengths due to a large amount of C–N-related structures on the HN-CQDs surface, changing the electron energy levels of the system, which was consistent with the above theoretical calculations. The same phenomenon was also reflected in the excitation-emission matrix, where overall, the redshift in the HN-CQDs was significantly greater than in the other samples. Additionally, extended research on the quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime of the above mentioned CQDs revealed that their fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield were directly proportional to their nitrogen content, with relevant data presented in Table 2 . (Average τ = (τ1*B1+τ2*B2)/(B1+ B2)).

In addition, the nitrogen-doped CQDs exhibited polarization properties in solution. Before evaluating polarized light emission materials, certain characterization definitions and mechanisms had to be quantified and better understood. The degree of polarization (DOP) serves as an important parameter that can be used to measure the polarization ratio, defined as:
(1) P(I2–I1)/(I2+I1),
where I2 and I1 denote the light emission intensities parallel and perpendicular to the chosen alignment orientation, respectively, I2 is the maximum intensity of the polarized, and I1 is the minimum [38]. The value of P must be between 0 and 1, with P = 0 indicating that the materials exhibit no polarized light emissions. Therefore, a higher P value is generally pursued for practical applications. The experimental setup is schematically depicted in Fig. 8 . The TLS-EQ-77 tunable light source was used as an excitation source, and a polarizer was placed between the sample and the detector. The motorized rotation stage rotated the polarizer, and the luminescence intensity changes were recorded by power meters.

The graph of luminescence intensity versus the angle of the polarizer was plotted, as shown in Fig. 9 , and Equation (1) was used to calculate DOP. This experiment, the excitation light source wavelength was set to 400 nm. Calculations revealed that the degrees of polarization (DOP) for CQDs with high, medium, and low nitrogen-doped content were 0.9231, 0.8648, and 0.7857, respectively. It indicates that the polarization changes in the luminescence of nitrogen-doped CQDs are correlated with the nitrogen-doped content. According to Fig. 9, the variation curve of polarized emission intensity is almost consistent with the sine function, and there is a significant phase difference between the variation curves of polarized emission intensity of these three types of quantum dots. The above difference was likely due to variations in the crystal size [34–37]. Different nitrogen doping ratios resulted in varying degrees of distortion within the internal lattice structure of the CQDs, which was supported by the above XRD and TEM results—the CQDs with high nitrogen content lattice size reduced and overall anisotropy enhanced. In addition, as mentioned above in theoretical calculations, doping a large number of graphite nitrogen atoms into graphite nitrogen will occupy the original position of the carbon atoms, affecting the spatial arrangement and orderliness of the aromatic rings. We inferred that the significant changes in polarization and phase difference are related to a positive correlation [38,39].

Our study demonstrated that varying levels of nitrogen doping in CQDs led to distinct structural, optical, and polarization properties. The LN-CQDs exhibited a diamond-like sp3 carbon dominant structure, the MN-CQDs displayed a balanced co-dominance of sp2 and sp3 carbon, and the HN-CQDs had a graphite-like sp2 carbon dominant configuration. The degree of nitrogen doping affected carbon hybridization in the CQD core and introduced differences in lattice distortion and functional group composition, as determined by XRD, TEM, FTIR, and XPS analyses. These structural differences were mirrored in the optical properties, with the HN-CQDs demonstrating more intense absorption peaks and significant redshifts in photoluminescence, corresponding to a higher degree of graphitic nitrogen. Additionally, the polarization characteristics of the CQDs were notably influenced by the nitrogen content, with the HN-CQDs displaying higher degrees of polarization and phase differences. These findings may be crucial for tailoring the properties of CQDs for specific applications in the display field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conflicts of interest or competing interests
ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing fnancial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to infuence the work reported in this paper.
Data underlying the results presented in this paper are not publicly available at this time but may be obtained from the authors upon reasonable request.
Not Applicable.

Siyuan Chen: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Xingchen Liu: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Sen Li: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yonggen Tan: Investigation, Methodology. Jingyan Yu: Methodology, Project administration. Chao Zhang: Software. Jun Feng: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial support from Southern University of Science and Technology and Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory Program under contract no2021B1212040001. This work was also supported by the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No.2021M701589). The authors thank Prof.Qihe Wei, Dr.Shufang Gao, Dr. Tao Lei, Mr.Zheng Zhang, Mr Zhiyuan Huang for insights and discussions on materials synthesis. And we also acknowledge the assistance of SUSTech Core Research Facilities.
